_note: Starter mnemonics for German vocab. Keyed by entry text. Never overwrites user-written mnemonics.
Hallo: Identical to English 'hello' — Hallo! Germans borrowed it directly. Easiest word in the list.
Tschüss: Sounds like 'choos' or 'tchoos'. Imagine choosing to leave — 'I choose to go, tschüss!' A breezy goodbye.
Danke: Sounds like 'DAHN-keh'. 'Donkey thanks you' — a grateful donkey saying danke 🫏. Or: 'dank' = moist in English, but danke = thanks.
Bitte: Sounds like 'BIT-teh'. 'Bitter? Bit of please!' — bitte means please AND you're welcome. A polite word that goes both ways.
Ja: Identical sound to English 'ya' (informal yes). Germans say 'ja' like Americans say 'yeah'. Done.
Nein: Sounds like English 'nine'. 'Cloud nine? Nein!' — No, not cloud nine, just nein (no) 🙅.
der Apfel: 'Apfel' sounds like 'apple' — nearly identical! The 'f' shifted to 'pp' over centuries. Der Apfel = the apple.
das Wasser: 'Wasser' looks like 'water' — same Germanic root! W→W, ss→t, er→er. Das Wasser flows.
das Buch: Sounds like 'bookh' — very close to 'book'! Same Germanic root. Buch/book diverged but stayed similar.
die Katze: Sounds like 'KAT-seh'. 'Katz' — like the surname Katz (Jewish surname meaning cat). Katze = cat 🐱.
der Hund: Sounds like 'hoont'. Same root as English 'hound' — a hunting dog. Hund = dog, hound = hunting dog.
essen: Sounds like 'ES-sen'. 'Essen' is also a city in Germany — imagine eating (essen) in Essen. To eat.
trinken: Sounds like 'TRINK-en'. 'Drink' is right there — trinken shares the same Germanic root as 'drink'. To drink.
groß: Sounds like 'grohs'. 'Gross!' in English means big/large informally ('gross exaggeration'). Groß = big.
klein: Sounds like 'kline'. 'Klein' is a common surname meaning small — think of the fashion brand Calvin Klein (small = refined).
gut: Sounds like 'goot'. Same root as English 'good' — gut/good. Direct cognate. Gut = good.
der Mensch: Sounds like 'mensh'. Yiddish 'mensch' means a good person — borrowed directly from German. Der Mensch = person.
der Freund: Sounds like 'froynd'. 'Freund' shares root with English 'friend' — fr-ie-nd / Fr-eu-nd. Same word, slightly different vowel shift.
der Student: Identical to English 'student' — der Student. German borrowed it from Latin just like English did.
der Lehrer: Sounds like 'LEHR-er'. 'Lehren' = to teach; '-er' = one who does it. A Lehrer is a teacher — one who teaches (lehrt).
arbeiten: Sounds like 'AR-by-ten'. 'Arbeit' = work — the root of 'Arbeit macht frei' (infamous phrase). Arbeiten = to work.
lernen: Sounds like 'LEHR-nen'. Very close to English 'learn' — same root. Lernen = to learn. Cognate.
mögen: Sounds like 'MUH-gen'. 'May I?' — mögen shares root with English 'may' (permission/possibility). To like/want.
schön: Sounds like 'shurn'. 'Shown beautifully' — something shown (schön) is beautiful. Schön = beautiful/nice.
glücklich: Sounds like 'GLOOK-likh'. 'Glück' = luck; '-lich' = -ly. Lucky = happy! Glücklich = happy/lucky.
schnell: Sounds like 'shnell'. 'Schnell!' is used in English movies for 'quick, move it!' — you probably already know this one.
langsam: Sounds like 'LAHNG-zahm'. 'Lang' = long; 'sam' = together. Slow = taking a long time. Langsam = slow.
kaufen: Sounds like 'KOW-fen'. 'Kauf' shares root with English 'cheap' (via Old English 'ceap' = trade/bargain). Kaufen = to buy.
das Geld: Sounds like 'gelt'. 'Gelt' is Yiddish/slang for money — borrowed from German Geld. Same word!
der Arzt: Sounds like 'artst'. Tricky one — comes from Greek 'archiatros' (chief healer). Arzt = doctor. Think: 'art of healing'.
die Wirtschaft: Sounds like 'VEERT-shahft'. 'Wirt' = innkeeper/host; '-schaft' = -ship. Economy = how a host manages resources.
obwohl: Sounds like 'ob-VOHL'. 'Ob' = whether; 'wohl' = well/probably. 'Whether well or not' = although. Obwohl = although.
verstehen: Sounds like 'fer-SHTAY-en'. 'Ver-' = prefix; 'stehen' = to stand. To understand = to stand [in someone's place]. Verstehen.
erklären: Sounds like 'ehr-KLEHR-en'. 'Er-' = prefix; 'klären' = to clarify (from 'klar' = clear). Erklären = to explain = to make clear.
die Sprache: Sounds like 'SHPRAH-kheh'. 'Sprache' shares root with 'speech' and 'speak' — Germanic cognate. Die Sprache = language/speech.
reisen: Sounds like 'RYE-zen'. 'Rise and travel' — reisen = to travel. Or: 'raise anchor and sail away'.
die Freiheit: Sounds like 'FRY-hyte'. 'Frei' = free; '-heit' = -ness/-hood. Freedom = free-ness. Freiheit = freedom. Cognate with English 'free'.
entscheiden: Sounds like 'ent-SHY-den'. 'Ent-' = away; 'scheiden' = to separate. To decide = to cut away alternatives. Entscheiden.
